CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The long-drawn-out fight for the recognition of Irish
as a subject of examination for Great S. and W. Rail-
“way Clerkships has at length resulted in the anti-Irish
directorate caving in. [Re the examination to be held
on July 16th the announcement concerning the subjects
of the examination contains the following:—“ Any two
of the following languages—Latin, Greek, French, Ger-
man, Irish.”” So Irish at last has forced its way in, no
thanks to Brother Goulding and his fellow amti-Irish
bigots who held out as long as they could.

Thie question of Irish was first raised as far back as
the meeting in February in 1903. At that meeting
‘Brother Goulding said that they could not afford, in

{ duct of the business of the company, to lend
selves to merely sentimental considerations! The

amendment to the report proposed at that meeting by
Mr. Michael Sullivan was lost, Brother Goulding pop-
ping out of his pocket, as it were, proxies against it te
the extent of £1,398,836 worth of stock.

At the following meeting in August, 1903, tihe Irish
question was raised again, and a lawyer by the name of
Dr. Falconer, K.C., wanted to know whether it would
be right to charge shareholders who might be English,
Scoteh, or American with keeping up the Irish language
through the medium of railway examinations? —This
profound lawyer ought to form an anti-Irishi and non-
TIrishi shareholders’ committee, now that the directors
have bowed their bigot hicads to the Irish demand. The
“ Irish Times,”” at the time commenting on the proceed-
ings at that meeting of the Great Sourface Railway, -
remarked—" As to the cry for the Irish language as a
subject, for examination for railway clerks, it is really
too ridiculous to be seriously debated.”’ Indeed! And
now this “really too ridiculous’’ arrangement is opera-
tive in the scheme of Examinations for clerkships in
the G. S. and W. Railway; and in the meantime the
“Irish Times’’ lhias subscribed to the Language Fund!

At the meeting in February, 1904, the word * Irish **
acted as a red rag to a bull on the Sourfaces present.
We wrote at the time:—“ When Mr. Sullivan brought
out the Irish question fair and straight the Bulls began
to bellow. We cannot recall that we ever witnessed
more ungentlemanly behaviour on the part of people
who, we assume, would claim to be men of some sense
of decorum and good manners. The elemental hate of
Ireland that is deep down in the lieart of the Sourface
broke out without restraint—a pack of corner-boys could
hardly behave worse.”” Alas! what do these screeching
Sourfaces think now that Irish is admitted by the
treacherous directors as a subject for examinations?

At the last meeting, the mecting hield on February of
this year, it may be remembered that the Rev. Dr.
Barrett, President of St. Colman’s College, Fermoy,
pointed out that if a Bill of the company were blocked
in Parliament by the Irish members by way of retalia-
tion for the boycott of Irish, it would mean considerable
expenditure to the company. We expressed the hope
that thie Irish Nationalist members of Parliament would
block the next Bill that the Great Sourface Railway
presented to Parliament unless the directors placed
Irish in the programme of their clerkship examinations.
Thle directors have done so. They were impervious to
reason ; they gave way under an intelligent anticipa-
tion of what we may call dynamic force to come!

The Dublin correspondent of tho “:Anglo-Celt’’
(Cavan), - discussing the International Exhibition, re-
marks :—“ Up to the present they (the Irish mamufac-
turers) are not advertising their wares, despite all that
the LEADER has written on the subject. They are the
Dark Brothenhood still. When they see lhow very much
better the manufacturers of other countries put up
their goods it will drelp the Irish to recognise their own
shortcomings. And they have many of them.”

In the course of a lecture delivered in the Oram N.S.,
Castleblayney, on “The Gaelic League and Ireland,’’
Father B. Maguire, C.C., according to the report in
our contemporary, the ¢ Anglo-Celt,” said :—* For, let
me here proclaim my candid opinion that the Dublin and
Belfast daily Press is one of the greatest calamities our
country |has to encounter, one of the greatest barriers
to the realisation of an Irish-Ireland. If the entire
plant and staff of the ‘ Freeman,’ ‘ Independent,’  Irish

THE LEADER.

Times,’ ‘Belfa.s!; News,” and all the other emanations
of porverted Irish genius were to-morrow buried deep
beneath the waters of the Irish Sea, Ireland would be
distinctly the gainer.’”

. Ha§ Humphrey’s Dump resolved to dump even foreign
cider into Ireland? On March 306h a firm of Tnish cider
manufacturers wrote to the Secretary of Humphrey's
Dump asking if he would be good enough' to send the
name apd address of the caterers to the Exhibition, as
they wished to endeavour to sell some cider to them.
On the 2nd April the “ Chief Exccutive Officer’’ re-
phed:—-" I am in receipt of your letter of the 30th ult.,
and in reply beg to inform you that the contract for
cider has been arranged.’”’

Now, we are only aware of the existence of three cider
manufacturers in Ireland—the Co. Armagh Cider Co.,
Portadown; Mr. Thomas Power, of Dungarvan, and
Messrs. Rutter, of Cork. None of these manufacturers
‘tlas got the contract direct. Of course, it is possible,
for all we know to the contrary, that a trading firm
tivat might buy Irish cider has got the contract. But
we doubt it. We don’t suppose it is any use asking the
© Chief Executive Officer ”’ for definite information on
the point.

We confess to an incapacity to enthuse over the
speech of the Eloquent Mr. Birrell. Mr. Birzell has a
sense of humour—a sense which we in Ireland sadly
lack—and he is a good-natured ttype of cynic. We are
sure he enjoys making eloquent speechles, and if his
delivery is as good as the quality of his words we arc
sure we would enjoy listening to him. But we, having
a eense of humour also, would certainly refrain from
making owrselves ridiculous by applauding him.

We think that the reason that clerks in-banks are
able to carry so much “side ’’ with the acquiescence of
the small urban populations of Ireland is that their
work is done in close proximity to the “tills,”” and these
money “tills ”’ caet a reflected glory on the clerks. For
we are a poor people, and heaven knows it is not with-
out some cause that we respect “tills,”” and 'those in
close proximity to them. The Eloquent Mr. Birrell is
in close proximity to the “#ill Mop’ over in Saxonland,
the great stocking by the name of “ The Treasury.”” He
can go in and ask ““The Treasury’’ for a handful of
gold, even tiough he may not get it, and may wink
knowingly at the Chancellor as he takes the latter’s re-
fusal; no wonder, perhaps, that the teachers cheered
him. “The entire assemblage at once rose,’’ we are
told, ““ and cheered as he took a seat on the right of the
President, Mr. Gamble.”” The Eloquent Mr. Birrell
has too much understanding to allow himself for a
moment to lay the flattering unction to his soul that it
was he wiho was being cheered ; it was the “ till Mmop”
that was being ihonoured and humoured. The President
of ithe teachers was obsequious; heaven help us, when
will we expel the half-slave out of us, and learn to
stand up straight? The President said :—“ It was the
first time in the history of their organisation that a
Minister of the Crown had condescended to discuss with
them in public the grave, the very grave and serious
question involved in the reform of primary education
in that country.”” We hlave italicised “ condescended
60 as to bring out the half-elave spirit of the introduc-
tion. Condescended, indeed! What does the Clalief
Secretary draw his huge salary for if not to
do the work of his office, and where is the
use of the primary teachers of Ireland flopping down,
or rathér jumping up, because this highly-paid offi-

APRIL 13, 1907.

cial—whose work hundreds would be prepared to do for
one quarter the pay—condescended’’ to regale them
with an “Eloquent ’’ speech? We suggest that that is
not the spirit that will impress a shrewd man like the
Bloquent Mr. Birrell; no doubt he sized up the matter
in his own mind as'he drove away in the “jaunting °
car.”” Tven the “jaunting car,”” no doubt, softened
the green hearts of his audience.

No “voice ’ asked “ what about the fees for Tvish?’
Eloquence is cheap, but what about the money, our
own money, Mr. Birrell?

The Eloquent Mx. B. said :— I think tbe Chancellor
of the Exchequer would admit of all his sturdy beggars
I am the sturdiest. Still he has many beggars knocking
at this door, and as T come in, I find another fellow steal-
ing out, and I sometimes hope without anything in his
pocket, so that there might be more left for me.”” The
teachers laughed at that plémap. We wonder would
that sort of stuff go down with an assembly of “ canny’’
Scots? We doubt it. ‘They would be keener on the
“bawbees,”” and would not be hushed to applause, if
we ‘may use the paradoxical phrase, by the soft
talk.  The eloquent and cynical Mr. Birrell con-
cluded his plamdp in this way:—“Now, ladies and
gentlemen, I can only thank you most cordially for the
very kind reception you have given to me, and I hope,
i¢f I remain in office a sufficient length of time, and f
things go on as T trust they will go, before long to come
before you, not only with sympathetic words, but with
full hands.”’” We have italicised the two “ifs.”” How-
ever, notwithstanding the two “ ifs,”’ the assembled mul-
titude broke into “loud applause.”” But what about
the fees for Irish?

Recently Mr. William Field, M.P., asked the P.M.G.,
Mr. Sydney Buxton, a question with reference to the
late importation from Scotland to the Postmastership
of Cashel. e wanted to know if there was no officer
in Ireland fitted for promotion to ‘Cashel, and in what
respect was the appointee the best qualified, and what
was the number of applications for the vacancy received
from officers in Ireland? Mr. Buxton steered clear of
the main points of the question, and gave a reply to the
effect, that the imported Sandy possessed all the qualifi-
cations required—he omitted to say what they were—-
and that in his opinion the lad from Scotland was the
most suitable candidate for the post. Of course, he was
—sure wasn't he from Scotland where the experts’
come from? He had longer service than the best quali-
fied of thle Irish candidates, Mr. Buxton stated. By
wihat means did Mr. Buxton arrive at this conclusion?
What test of fitness was applied? What was ithe length
of service of the senior Tvish official applying, and to
what office attached?

Some time ago we pointed out that Tredegar, in
Wales, required a Postmaster. We predicted that the
vacancy would be filled by an official serving in Wales,
and we were right. The language question had to be
considered.  We find that the “salaried Sub-Post-
master *’—we were under the impression that all Post-
masters, sub as well as head, were salaried—of Aber-
tillery, Newport (Mon.), has been appointed to the
vacancy. When will the knowledge of Trish be an essen-
tial on the part of candidates applying for Irish offices?

Still the importations come! The Postmastership of
Birr was rec.ently vacant. We find that a Mr. C. A.
Simon, described as an overseer in Southport—which we

We' would commend this Dump to the attention of

be.\r of candidates that applied for the Birr vacancy, and
of thkse how many came from officers serving in Ire-

service of the senior in each instance, and to what'offices
they were attached. We venture to predict thiat the
most senior man is attached to an Irish office.

Th? financial condition of the Irish Drapers’ Assis-
tants' Beunefit and Protective Associations appears to be
very strong. We read in ithe fifth annual report that

- the total income for 1906 amounted to £2,977 12s. 1d.,

the expenditure, including £1,077 17s: 11d. paid in
benefits, to £2,166 2&: TBhe total net balance to tlhe
credit of the Association was £3,180 8s. 9d. Five new
branches were formed; and 934 new members were
enrolled. The total efféctive membership was' 3,166.

The Keating Branch of the Gaelic League would
apparently have a greater amount of faith in the Elo-
quent Mr. Birrell’s promises if the country and the
Irish members of Parliament kept up pressure. At are-
cent meeting of the branch the following resolution was
passed unanimously—“That we view with satisfaction the
promise of Mr. Birrell that the fees for teaching Irish in
the National Scibols: will be restored, but bearing in
mind the attitude of the Treasury on questions relating
to education in Ireland, we appeal to the country and
to the Irish members of Parliament not to relax their
effcrts until effect is given to the promise by thee adoption
of the programme approved of by the National Board
of the Coiste Gnotha of the Gaelic League, or the alter-
native programme of the Gaelic Union of National
Teachers.’’

Father Dinneen is not, to say the least, head and ears
in love with the British “ Liberal ’ Government. He
ventures to think, and we venture to agree with him,
that the “Liberal”” Government has been decidedly
mean concerning the fees for Irish. In the course of
his address at the Keating Branch, he said:—“The
withdrawal of the fees by the Tories gave their suc-
cessors, who are called Liberals, an opportunity of show-
ing how far they would go to satisfy Irish sentiment,
of showing how they were prepared to supply a crying
educational need. How did the Liberals use this oppor-
tunity? Tt is a matter of history that they have dealt
with this question in a spirit of indescribable meanness,
of shuffling, of vague speeches of sympathy, culminat-
ing in the establishing of a scale of payment so beggarly
as to constitute an insult to the teachers of Ireland, to
the people of Ireland, to' the language of Ireland, which

“ The Universe’’ of London describes itself as “ The
oldest popular Catholic newspaper.”’ It was started in
1860, about 47 years ago. It is publishing a series of
articles under the heading “ Our Social Forces,” and
the article in its issue of April 5th is on ““ The Gaelic
League as a Social Force.”” The article, which runs: to
five columus, is very interesting. In the course of the

article we read :—“ This is not the place to enter into
the question of the general apathy that pervaded Irish
life before the Gaelic League roused the slumbering
people to new efforts. Those who wislh will find an able
discussion of the subject in the illuminating pages of
Mr. Moran’s ‘ Philosophy of Irish Ireland.” Here it
will suffice to say bhat in the course of long struggles
thle national ideal had become somewhat weakened and
debased. A loss of confidence in themselves, a slavish
straining after imitation of foreign manners and foreign
civilisation, had produced the very worst effects upon
the minds of the people. Dispirited by frequent disap-
pointmeat they despaired of ever bettering their condi-
tion, and the only prospect before Ysem was emigration
to distant lands.”

Who compose the “ Clonmel Amateur Dramatic Com-
pany?’  Whoever they are, they are touring round
‘“Gallant > Tip. They are to perform, or have per-
formed, at Carrick, where the famous dogs come from,
Cashel of the Kings and Koons, Clonmel, Tipperary,
and Ardfinan. We hear that all the printing in con-
nection with this tour has been procured from England.
Certainly at the end of one of the posters issued by this
company is.the imprint of a Chatham firm of printers.

A correspondent writes:—“In the current number
of the Leaber I was particularly struck by your list of
books as a nucleus for a. parish library. I think it is as
good a selection as could be made—of course, none
would seem ideally satisfactory. But I don’t think you
ought to have ignored altogether in this context the pub-
lications of the Catholic Truth Society. These publica-
tion are by no means intended to be sold only in boxes
at Churchi doors. They are exceedingly cheap—a reason
for including and recommending them, surely, not, as
I think sometimes happens, for Tuokiug down on them,
as if a book that is sold at 1d. may not easily and often
be as good as another sold at 6d. or at 2s. 6d. I take
the liberty of enclosing you a list of Irish Catholic
Truth publications, marking some of the more striking
¢ Irish Ireland ’ ones.”

The chief, and indeed the only, reason that we did
not include any of the penny booklets of the Iridh
Catholic Truth Society was that their size and  get up”’
render them unsuitable for a circulating library. It
was suggested to us, and we think with sound reason,
that books for a library should at least have stiff cloth
binding. We would be sorry, indeed, to suggest that
Lecause a book only costs a penny that it is not of great
worth. But besides being unsuitable for the purpose of
a circulating library a penny book is probably one that
people even of very slender means would rather pur-
chase than go to the trouble to take a loan from a
library. Our correspondent marks the following in the
catalogue he has sent us : —*“ The Round Towers and the
Holy Wells of Ireland,”” by his Grace the Archbishop
of Tuam; “The Four Masters,”’ by the same author;
“The Boyne Valley: Its Antiquities and Ecclesiastical
Remains,” by J. B. Cullen; “J.K.L.—A Great Irish
Bishop,”” by Rev. P. Coakley, O.S.A.; “Montalem-
bert,” by J. J. Horgan; “Ireland as the Teacher of
England and Scotland,”” by Rev. George O’Neill, S.J.;
“From Peterto Leo,”’ by Rev. Robert Kane, S.J. ; _"The
Popes as International Peace-Makcrs,”lby Monsignor
O’Riordan ; “ The Irish Church in the S‘l}.Cbh Century,‘"
by Rev. E. A. D’Alton, C.C.; “The Irish Church in
tho Seventh and Eighth Centuries,” by the same
author;  Civilisation of Ireland Before the Anglo-
Norman Invasion,”” by Cardinal Moran ; “ Tara—Pagan
and Christian,” by Most Rev. Dr. Healy; “St. Patrick

. We note, by the way, that the Catholic Truth Society
issued a series of volumes bound.in cloth at one shilling
each containing several of the publications of the
Society. These volumes would, of course, be suitable
for library purposes, and we will include some, if not all,

we yet have to select.

‘We have before us a couple of examination papers
set at the recent ““King’s Scholarship > Examinations
that were conducted in the Cork “ Model ’ School. One
of the papers is on “ General Information.”” Here are
some of the questions—“ What do you understand by
(@) Cowper-Temple Teaching? (b) the West Riding Judg-
ment?’  Another is, ‘ Describe the Victoria Cross
For what is it awarded?’ Another, “ To what persons
are the following terms applied—Nihilists, Socialists,
Devolutionists?””  We wonder is there supposed to be a
brilliant joke in this connecting of poor, mild “ Devolu-
tionists ** with Nihilists? In the paper on ‘‘ History
of Great Britain and Ireland’ there is not very much
about Ireland. In one question students are invited to
write a note on “ Fidei Defensor,”” but comments are
not asked for on the interesting subject of the “anti-
Idolator.”

article in the “ Donegal Vindicator *’ of April 5th com-
mences in this way:—“ Has Ballyshannon definitely
receded from the position which it took up some three
years ago in connection with' the Language and Irish

show that it has lost whiatever small measure of patriotic.
spirit it ever possessed, and at no time could this have

and Language Week passed without a shilling being

An Ivish lady, residing in the South of England,
writes :— Dear Sir—I must apologise very much for
troubling you, but if you or any of your readers could
inform me where in Ireland I can get a pedigree Irish
terrier pup, over distemper, I should be so grateful. It
sounds an absurd request, but I have no friends who
keep dogs in Ireland now, and absolutely no knowledge
of the names and addresses of any large kennel owners
in the Emerald Isle. I see the LEADER every week, and
so turn to it from my exiled position over here, thoping
its editor may show me the same courtesy 'he recently
extended to another feminine inquirer who sought to
know the way to obtain ladies’ tweeds and cloths of
Irishi manufacture.”

THE NURSERY AND IRELAND.

¢¢ IDLAYING to the Gallery” is probably a

universal game in every part of the world.
The temptation to play to the gallery attends the life
of every public man now and again We are not free
from it in this country. But there is a particular temp-
tation in this abnormal land that, so far as we are
aware, does not operate in any other country in the
world, and that is the temptation to play to the nursery.
Some of the very young are keen politicians, and the
middle-aged have partly drifted into West Britonism
and apathy, and the old are at rest with thie local papers
and the reports of their Poor Law Board. Though our
very young politicians have not votes they have voices,
and they take themselves very seriously. The political
exttravagance of youth tends to intensify the prevailing
apathy of middle-aged Ireland, and the old shake tieir
heads at it, and the effect is that the very young have
a disproportionate share in Irish politics. A conse-
quence springing from this is—and one-that further con-
founds an abnormal situation already confused enough
in all conscience—that many politicians are tempted to
play to the nursery; and they yield to the temptation.
Paper: programmes hot discussions on theories, splits
as to whether we will regard Grattan’s Parliament or

. an Irish Republic as the ultimate goal of a country which

refuses to do its own insurance, and make its own boots,
distract the mind of very young Ireland, whilst middle-
aged Ireland, that has left its period of political
adolescence behind it, reads the imported English: Sun-
day papers, and goes occasionally to Dan Lowry’s and
to Dan Tallon’s, and is firmly convinced that the
youngsters of the theories, having sowed their wild
views, will settle down to apatlhly and West Britonism
like themselves.

We ondeavoured to make, and did make, the Irish
Ireland propaganda masculine and interesting to mature
minds, we played to the mabtured man, and to the
younger ones with level heads; others have succumbed
to the temptation to play to the nursery, and have pro-
duced a lob of serious youngsters lacking alike experience
and humour, and calculated to confirm others in their
apathy so far-as Ireland is concerned. Young people
aro interested in hysterics, toys, balloons and rockets;

THE LEADER.

mx.tu;x-e people are reflective, and have left their child-
hood’s wonder behind them.  If tthey are to be interested

‘in Treland their interest must be awakened through
tiheir reflective powers; the crude,. though genuine,
g}lhhusmsm of the nursery will not command their atten-
ion.

We have published two very interesting articles on
the want of a boys’ paper. We must say we have onc
or two boys’ papers already, though they are not suit-
able for the ordinary boy, they only fll' a want so far
as the prematurely serious and solemn political boy is

. concerned—the boy who would be tempted to strike his
companion not over a dispute about a bird’s nesting
expedition, but as to whether an English Act of Par-
liament in 1783 was or was not the oharter of Irish
liberty.

This tendency that exists in this abnormal country to
play tto the nursery has not, so far as we know, pre-
viously been noted as a distinctive phenomenon of
cur present situation. It is, however, we submit, an
important factor in it. There are two classes of
ycungsters—one have old heads of a sort on very young
shoulders, and the other have very young heads on
middle-aged, or even old, shoulders.  Some of the poli-
ticians who ought to know better have not the courage
to keep the youngster in the nursery, and the price the
country has to pay is that many of thie mature people
tend to remain in apathy and West Britonism.

CUTTING THE COLLEGE KNOT.

HAVE not hesitated to express my personal pre-
ference for a Royal University solution in the ques-

tion of Higher Education, and yet, on the other hand,
tihe imperative duty that lies upon all of us Catholics to
accept any reasonable proposal that may be offered. The
“sclution ’ game has been played against us with too

much ekill for too long, it being alternately represented
that any solution not taking in Trinity must be rejected
as denominational, as not including Protestants; and,
on the otller hand that any solution that did include
Protestants must be put aside because it affected Trinity

College. There has been all along a sort of thimble-
rigging, the {hree thimbles being marked * Catholic
TUniversity,”’” “Royal University,”” and “ Dublin Univer-
sity 7’ respectively. We have lifted two of them, and
found nothing. —Now that we are advancing on the
third, the gentlemen behind the table seem highly
excited, and keep calling out—* Wouldn’t your rever-
ence get him to try one of the other thimbles again?”
Let our reply be—“T don’t mind about the thimble,
but this time I'm going to have that pea.'’

Now if we only had a Parliament in College Green,
someone will tell you, it would have passed a law, and
settled the matter long ago. Of course it would. But
wiiat nobody seems to realise is that, it has already done
so. The Parliament in College Green has passed a law for
founding not a Catholic college, but a college for
Catholics. It is on our statute book at the present
moment, quite as much' in force as, say, Mr. Balfow’s
Coercion Act. * Lord Aberdeen (acting on Mr. Birrell’s
direction, of course) could create such a college before
breakfast any morning. And why should not he do so?
T once treated the matter in a slightly humorous vein
in these columns; I now ask the question in all
Seriousness.

Tt seoms to me that if the Cabinet have decided on
placing a second college in Dublin University as they
thave announced, they should, as far as the college for
Catholics is concerned, proceed under ithe Irish statute.
There is nothing in the least uncongtitutional or even
unusual in their putting these powers in force.  The
stabute has (as I pointed out in a former article) gained
special legislative recognition as late as 1829, so that
ac Acts of Parliament dating from the reign of King
Edward III. have frequently been enforced after long
periods, the argument from desuetude—an argument
never admitted.in the law either of England or of Ire-
Jland—fails entirely. The Act is clearly available. A:
second college can be placed in Trinity any day; the

APrIL §3. 1907.

only difficulty being ithat the endowment for it will
have to be secured by a subsequent money bill.

Dr. Pye has suggested a few days since the course I
venture now to recommend. Since the Government has
decided on a Dublin University solution there appear
t) be immense advantages in proceeding by the method
of first utilising the Irish Act, and subsequently going
for the endowment. We often hear of Irishmen manag-
ing Irish affairs. Well, Grattan’s Parliament thas
managed this affair considerably better than their suc-
cessors at Westminster. Nor are the interests of the
Protestant religion likely to have been unduly neglected
by the gentlomen who sat in‘ College Green. Again,
Trinity College claims that it must not be interfered
with owing to its amtiquity, but it cannot on that
ground object to a liability wihich has been an incident
of its tenure for more than a century. “Hands off
Trinity I’” cry one or other of its roving defenders. Cer-
ttainly all ‘hands save those of the famous eighteenth
century Prolestants, we hear so much about, we reply,
smiling sweetly the while. For nothing is needed save
thle permission of the Castle to give Catholics a chare
in that renowned prestige, if not in that ill-got wealth.

To proceed thus under the Irish Act seems to me in
itself the reasonable and proper course, the least objec-
tionable in matter of form. But as a matter of tactics
it is yet more desirable. Everyone recognises that if
the Devolution Bill is to go on, to pass a detailed Uni-
versity measure this session, even through the Commons,
may prove well-nighan impossibility. —And the gang of
peers is always waiting round the corner. But a clause in
a money bill giving power to endow an existing college
would be a much easier matter, and comparatively safe
from the Lords’ attacks.  Or if the worst came to the
worst the college might be founded this year, and'the
Bill passed next, the college being meanwhile conducted
out of private resources or by indirectly utilising scme
of the R.U.I. revenues, as outlined in my former
article. A Bill to include Queen’s College, Belfast, in
Dublin University, if introduced later, would be much
harder to oppose ; and it is mere foolish pedantry which
at present refuses Cork the University charter that it
desires.

But, apart from all constitutional and political
reasons, there is, it seems to me, another immense advan-
tage in first founding your college under the Irish Act,
and then going to Parliament for its endowment. It
is always so much easier to defend an existing institu-
tion.  What chance would there be of establishing a
college like Trinity if it were attempted at present? It
would not be tolerated for a moment. But becavce it
has existed so long no one seems to wish its destruction.
And in like manner it will be much easier to defend
an endowment for the Catholic college when it is once
wn sitw.  For any number of absurd lies will be beiieved
about it as long as it is a future entity. But they can-
not survive in the clear light of actuality.

Entirely Catholic? Wihat about Mr. X and Dr. Y,
etc.? Rules by the bishops? How many are on the
governing body?  Catholics have no teaching staff?
Come and see. Science not taught? How did A get
the studentship in Biology, and Y read that paper on
ions? Low standard?  What about the General
Medical Council report, and the Home Civil Service
results? Walk in, gentlemen, and see for yourselves.
Once the thing is actually there in working order, or
even ready to start working, the fables vanish, and the
claim for endowment of a college founded by Grattan’s
Parliament, supported by three-fourths of the Irish
people and staffed with the best and most progressive
Irish talent, becomes irresistible. And the cry of
Trinity against its brother college, if it pevsist, will be
looked on as mere empty bigotry. I am no enthusiast
for thie Trinity connection. Bub if that is the solution
to be adopted there is only one sensible way of carrying
it out. CHANEL.

THE GRADUATE AND THE COMMERCIAL MAN.

“ROM time to time one is struck by the remarks of
would-be philosophers in reference to the alleged
antagonism between Higher Education and the busi-
ness faculty.  Their remarks practically amount to
this—that when you endow with higher education a
4 man whose natural place is in the world of commerce,
you have thereby for all practical purposes ruined that
I man and unclassed him. I am afraid that this criticism
i generally originates with two classes: the professional
men who feel their professions, to their prejudice, over-
¥ crowding, and the business men, who, through natural
utility and in spite of defective education, have
achieved some success in the commercial world. The
former would prefer that higher education were re-
stricted to the classes and not the masses. The latter
cannot see any benefit to be gained by over-educating—
to use their own phrase—the masses of the people.
To both these classes—and they are both numerous—
the University graduate is an object of abhorrence when
he enters their domain

We read with admiration of men originally handi-
capped by want of education who have made a mark
in the world, and who, refusing to devote all their
energies to the continued service of Mammon, give up
a part to the pursuit of self-culture. The self-made
man, who is' likewise a cultured man, is an object of
reverence. In these cases, however, the man usually
first worships at the altar of Mammon, and when its
worship has brought him success, turns aside a little
to devote himself in a limited way at the shrine of
culture.  There is another class of man—to me far
more admirable—who, at the very outset of his career,
keeps before this mind his dual nature. The man I
would indicate is the one who seeks to develop the body
by physical exercise, the mind by study, and to gain
for himself a position by hard work and close attention
to duty. Such a one seems to me a perfect man. Now
there are plenty such—young fellows in the ordinary
walks of life, unendowed with means, unblessed with
opportunity, who after their day’s toil spend their
evening in study and thus often become University
- graduates. What is their reward? They have unfitted

" themselves, moryah! for success in business pursuits.
‘“Tife is a comedy to those who think ”’—is this not
the keynote of the situation? The man who looks be-

My reply to “ Catholic Headmaster ”’ has evoked an
extraordinary display of Miltonian pyrotechnics. Apart
from this effect, “ Catholic Headmaster’’ appears in a
new réle quite distinet from his authority as an educa-
tionist.  The inventive genius of really great men
isolates them from those of more humble abilities, and
differentiates them by epecial characteristics, forming
a class apart, the aristocracy of intellect. Thus we find
Cuvier, the great paleontologist, could describe the un-
discovered megatherium from the study of a single
molar of that leviathan. This, considering the very
trifling data, was a feat of penetration, skill, and acute
reasoning seldom surpassed. The inventive genius of
the Yankee whose mechanical skill enabled him to con-
struct a barrel round a bung hole, would, if the account
were true, vie with the great French naturalist’s ex-
ploit. But Cuvier and our Western friend must come
but a poor second in comparison with our mew light, the
“ Catholic Headmaster.”  He, by the sheer dint of
genius, has performed the extraordinary feat of de-
termining a man’s character from a single aspirate. The
problem was one of extreme intricacy—given the letter
“H” in a nom de plume, determine the writer’s dhar-
acter.  His analytical result ‘is worth repeating.
“ Smooth hypocrisy, dog-in-the-manger policy, unable
or unwilling to educate boys beyond 16, etc.” Such
are some traits of “H’s’’ character logically derived
from his nom de p'ume, and essentially derivable there-
from. Considering this feat; Mark Twain’s fowl, after
her parturitive exploit, need not do all the loud cack-
ling, and were she alive (for obstetrical science has
its limitations) and capable of reading the LEADER of
March 23rd, I feel certain her cachinnations would be
pitched in a more modest key.

Not alone his constructive, but his destructive powers
are on a scale equally grand. Poor “ Fair Play ’’ fired
but a single bolt, he says, and fled. Perhaps he has
taken to bed and died of laughter at  Catholic Head-
master’s’’ logic. Fitzjames, placing his back against
the rock, and bravely crying:—

tions from gentlemen like Mr. O’Sullivan, to whom
the younger generation naturally look up to dissipate
this belief. I must be permitted not to share absolutely
his theory of the vowel sounds in Italian. It cer-
tainly was news to me to learn that each vowel in that
language has a double value. This is not so, or if it is
a fact, I have never been able to detect this dual sound
of each vowel in the mouth of either a Tuscan or a
Roman, and I have spoken with natives of both
provinces in their own country. I am aware that E
and O do sometimes in Italian get what appears to the
uninitiated a short sound, but this occurs only in a
few, very few words indeed. I have an Italian book
open before me, “ The Letters of Leopardi,”” and it is
only after very careful scrutiny I can discover in half
a dozen pages a few words in which I remember hearing
in Ttaly this short vowel sound: “luogo,”” “ho,”
“oggi,”” “bello,”” ““benuenuto,” and even in these the
most striking instances of the very few cases in which
the short vowel sound obtains, or may be said to
obtain ; the long vowel sound may be used in every in-
stance without offending the very fastidious Italian ear.
I think I have said enough in this connection to
prove that the grounds on which Mr. O’Sullivan
claims the Irish to be written more phonetically than
the Italian, are very flimsy, and will not bear discus-
sion. What is the reason which he assigns for the fre-
quent, occurrence of those silent and wholly irrelevant
letters in Irish words, which offend the eye so much,

and dismay a student of thie very hottest ardour? The
reason he gives is this, and it is one well worthy of
tho oracular Dogberry:—No two or three vowels
joined to each other in the same word can form two
separato syllables.”” And why not, pray? The fact of
the matter is that such has already been done, and is
being done by Mr. O’Sullivan himself everytime he
writes Irish. I am aware that bionn does nob fiod
favour generally, still I doubt if an 5ditbeanac him-
self writes broeann. If we do not get two syllables
in Sceal, cuan, aon, caol, Lao¢, what in the name
of Abbe Rosisel@t do we get; and that without the aid
of your adventitious and unsightly  or v stuck in
the middle, like the pig in the post cards of the Irish-
man about which we grumble. The instance which we
got is glavac, let us hope this is a misprint, aod
shirk from attributing it to ignorance, as Mr. O Su]h-
van has done so promptly a slip on another occasion
to “Chanel.”” The word is <lao0a¢. and would not
the word look far nicer and much less formidable and
puzzling it we were to write steoc. Why does
&n Suilbedna¢ write stdovac¢ and nob write Laovac
for Lao¢. The truth is that Irish is encumbered by its
spelling, and “Chanels” berv and ma¢ remain un-
answered. When will this Chinese prejudice for old
forms give way to the modern spirit of simplicity? I
should like to know what the Italians would say if Mr.
O’Sullivan was to try to introduce his adventitious
letters into their own beautiful tongue. The Ttalian
is not alone the most beautiful tongue in existence to-
day, but is also the most phonetic. A person ac-
quainted with both tongues, and who would compare a
page of Forcolo with a page of Keating, and then
assert that Keating wrote more phonetically than Ugo,
is surely suffering from a hallucination. '

A radical change must be made sooner or later in the
spelling of Trish if the youth of the land is expected to
devote itself diligently to the learning of their native
tongue. The apathy which is attributed to young
people who at Gne time have thought of doing a primary
duty to their country, and who have given up after a
short, experience of Gaelic League classes, is not apatiy,
it is absolute despair of ever being able to make rhyme
or reason of its spelling. ~ Wihen a reform in this is
made we shall march on; meantime Father O’Leary is
pounced on as a crank because he dares to tear himself
ever such a small bit away from the conventional.

The latter part of An Suitbeanac's defence
laughable. It appears that down here somew!
the County Cork a few native speakers managed to spell
their way through in the first of Miss Borthwick’s books
in three nights; they are forthwithiheld up to the admi-
ration of the multitude as instances of what can be done
to master the terrible orthography. M. .O’Sullivan
gravely asserts that the parties learned to read Irish in
three lessons, it may, with as much reason, be asserted
by a grandfather that his son is able to read English
because he can read “Tom is on the Moor’’ and the
other lessons in his first book.

Sedn Mac Mupcava.
